Machine learning learns the same way you do: by looking at examples. 
It studies past data such as: 
• weather 
• photos 
• words you’ve typed 
Then it looks for patterns and turns those patterns into simple rules the computer can follow.
Those rules become your machine learning model.

📊 Analyze Data: Excel now interprets your entire dataset spotting trends, outliers, and correlations instantly.
It’s like having a built-in data analyst.

☁️ Cloud Glossary Starter Pack: 

• Cloud Computing = Services over the internet 
• Cloud storage = Save data online 
• Containers = Portable app packages 
• DevOps = Combine development and operations for faster delivery 
• Hybrid Cloud = Mix of on-premises infrastructure + Cloud 
• IaaS = Infrastructure as a service = On-demand IT infrastructure 
• Migration = Moving apps/data to cloud 
• Multi-Cloud = Use multiple providers 
• Networking = Connect systems and apps in the cloud

Not all clouds work the same. To understand cloud computing, it helps to know the 4 cloud models:

Public Cloud → Like renting a room. You get your own space in a shared building.
Private Cloud → Like owning a house. Full control & responsibility to maintain it.
Hybrid Cloud → A house + apartment combo. Keep sensitive work at home & use the apartment when you need to scale.
Multi‑Cloud → Renting rooms in different buildings. More flexibility across providers, but more complexity to manage.

New to AI? Understand the difference between generative AI, and AI agents: 

1️⃣ Generative AI creates new content like text, images, or code based on patterns in data. 
For example: Drafting emails, generating images, or writing code snippets. 
2️⃣ AI agents combine generative AI with tools, memory, and rules to complete tasks across different steps. 
For example: An agent can pull data, summarize it, and send a report automatically.

AI security is everyone’s responsibility. Protect AI innovation with 5 steps: 

✅ Build a security team for AI
✅ Optimize resources to secure GenAI
✅ Take a Zero Trust approach
✅ Partner with trusted vendors
✅ Adopt a comprehensive AI security solution

Here at Microsoft, we're at the cutting edge of using AI to solve real work problems. In our HR department, we noticed that manual reports & fragmented tools slowed teams down. So we mapped the workflow to understand which steps AI could support. The team broke it into clear tasks: pull data, summarize trends, draft the first version. You’re already using these security concepts in your daily workflow, even if you don’t use the formal terms. Save this for when you want to brush up.

👉 MFA → An extra check after your password
👉 Encryption → Your data, locked into unreadable code
👉 Zero trust → Every request gets checked, no exceptions
👉 Phishing → Fake messages trying to steal your info
👉 Least privilege → Access limited to only what you truly need

☁️ Cloud Glossary Starter Pack: 

• Cloud Computing = Services over the internet 
• Cloud storage = Save data online 
• Containers = Portable app packages 
• DevOps = Combine development and operations for faster delivery 
• Hybrid Cloud = Mix of on-premises infrastructure + Cloud 
• IaaS = Infrastructure as a service = On-demand IT infrastructure 
• Migration = Moving apps/data to cloud 
• Multi-Cloud = Use multiple providers 
• Networking = Connect systems and apps in the cloud 
• PaaS = Platform as a service = Managed app development and deployment 
• SaaS = Software as a service = Ready-to-use software 
• Scalability = Flex resources based on demand 
• Security = Protect cloud data, apps and infrastructure 
• Serverless = Run code without managing servers 
• VM = Virtual machine = Software-based OS running on hardware

Ever nodded along in an AI conversation, only to look things up afterward? This guide can help. 📖 
• AI model → A system trained on data to detect patterns and generate output
• Compute → The computing power and resources needed to train and run AI models 
• Context window → The amount of information an AI model can consider at once 
• Fine-tuning → Adjusting a pre‑trained model using specific training data so it performs better on a particular task
• Neural network → The layered structure that allows AI models to learn complex patterns
• Token → A unit of text the AI model processes, such as a word or part of a word
• Training data → Information used to train and improve AI systems
• Zero-shot learning → The ability of an AI model to perform tasks it was not explicitly trained on

Not all clouds work the same. To understand cloud computing, it helps to know the 4 cloud models:

Public Cloud → Like renting a room. You get your own space in a shared building.
Private Cloud → Like owning a house. Full control & responsibility to maintain it.
Hybrid Cloud → A house + apartment combo. Keep sensitive work at home & use the apartment when you need to scale.
Multi‑Cloud → Renting rooms in different buildings. More flexibility across providers, but more complexity to manage.

🚀 AI, career pathways, and the future of higher education.

Day one at SXSW EDU centered on how AI is reshaping learning and career preparation in higher education.

A theme that came up repeatedly was career pathways. Students want clearer connections between what they study and the jobs they can pursue. More than 15 million young people explore career paths each year, which shows how many learners are actively trying to understand what roles exist and what skills they’ll need.

Another major conversation focused on workforce readiness. As AI changes how work gets done, educators are thinking differently about the capabilities students need to graduate with. Skills like problem solving, digital fluency, adaptability, and collaboration with AI systems are becoming foundational.

One session showed how classrooms are beginning to integrate AI directly into learning. Students aren’t just studying the technology. They’re learning how to prompt, collaborate with, and think critically alongside AI tools and agents.

The takeaway from day one: the next generation of learners will use AI and lead with it.

Higher education is evolving so students graduate with both the skills and career awareness needed for an AI-driven workforce.

New to AI? Understand the difference between generative AI, and AI agents: 

1️⃣ Generative AI creates new content like text, images, or code based on patterns in data. 
For example: Drafting emails, generating images, or writing code snippets. 
2️⃣ AI agents combine generative AI with tools, memory, and rules to complete tasks across different steps. 
For example: An agent can pull data, summarize it, and send a report automatically.

Save this now, and revisit in January to start strong. For now, think about these three steps:
1️⃣Choose one free Microsoft resource.
Ask yourself:
• What do I want to be better at in the next 3–6 months?
• Am I learning for my current role, or preparing for what’s next?
• Do I need structure (guided learning) or exploration (hands-on practice)?
• How much time can I realistically give each week?
This helps you decide between:
• Microsoft Learn (structured paths)
• AI Skills Navigator (direction and mapping)
• Copilot (practice and experimentation)
• Docs (depth and reference)
2️⃣Join a community.
Ask yourself:
• Do I stay motivated learning alone, or with others?
• Would seeing real examples help me understand faster?
• Do I learn best by asking questions or observing discussions?
• Am I looking for inspiration, answers, or accountability?
This points you toward:
• Microsoft Tech Community
• Virtual or in-person Microsoft events
• Learning challenges and shared spaces
3️⃣Let consistency do the rest.
Ask yourself:
• What pace can I maintain without burning out?
• When during my week does learning actually fit?
• What would “showing up” look like for me right now?
• How will I know this is working, even in small ways?
Consistency looks like:
• Short, repeatable sessions
• Progress over perfection
• Letting skills compound over time

You don’t need pressure.
You need clarity and a rhythm you can keep.
